presentation.title: WHAT IS BIRDWATCHING?
presentation.visual: A person standing on a lush green hiking trail, holding binoculars up to their eyes and looking up into the canopy of trees.
presentation.text: Birdwatching, or birding, is the simple act of observing birds in their natural habitats. It invites us to slow down and observe the wildlife thriving around us.

presentation.title: FOR EVERYONE, EVERYWHERE
presentation.visual: A diverse group of people of varying ages sitting on a park bench in a bustling city setting, looking closely at a small sparrow on the sidewalk.
presentation.text: You do not need to travel far or be an expert. Birding can happen anywhere, from remote national parks to your own backyard or local city street.

presentation.title: THE CLOSER LOOK
presentation.visual: A close-up view of a pair of black binoculars resting on an old wooden log in a sunny forest setting.
presentation.text: While not strictly required, a good pair of binoculars is the most essential tool for a birder. They bring distant, small details right in front of your eyes.

presentation.title: NAME THAT BIRD
presentation.visual: A hand holding a smartphone displaying a bird identification app, with a colorful songbird perched on a branch slightly out of focus in the background.
presentation.text: Field guides and modern smartphone apps help you identify what you are seeing and hearing, turning every outing into a fun learning experience.

presentation.title: USE YOUR EARS
presentation.visual: A person standing completely still in a dense forest, eyes closed, listening intently with a peaceful expression.
presentation.text: Birding is often half-listening. Before you see a bird, you will frequently hear its song, its call, or the rustle of leaves as it searches for food.

presentation.title: SCAN FOR MOVEMENT
presentation.visual: A dense cluster of green leaves on a tree branch, with a slight blur suggesting the sudden movement of a small bird shifting between twigs.
presentation.text: Keep your eyes peeled for sudden movement. A flickering shadow, a swaying leaf, or a bouncing branch is often the best clue that a bird is nearby.

presentation.title: THE FOUR KEYS TO ID
presentation.visual: A divided visual showing a bird silhouette, a close up of vibrant wing feathers, a bird eating seeds, and a marshy wetland.
presentation.text: When identifying a bird, note four key details: its overall size and shape, its distinct color pattern, its behavior, and the habitat it is in.

presentation.title: WHERE TO LOOK
presentation.visual: A scenic sunrise over a marsh wetland with a wooden boardwalk extending into the tall grass.
presentation.text: National Parks, local nature reserves, wetlands, and coastal shores offer rich habitats, making them incredible destinations for birdwatchers.

presentation.title: BIRDING ETHICS
presentation.visual: A faraway view of a bird's nest in a tree trunk, with a person standing far back on a marked trail looking through binoculars.
presentation.text: Always respect wildlife. Keep a generous distance, stay on marked trails, and never disturb nests or roosting areas just to get a closer look.

presentation.title: WHY WE BIRD
presentation.visual: A smiling person pausing on a bright trail, lowering their binoculars with a look of awe and deep relaxation.
presentation.text: Birdwatching promotes mindfulness, reduces stress, and invites a deeper appreciation for the delicate ecosystems that surround us.
